title: Critical surface strain regime for stable and active epitaxial platinum oxygen reduction electrocatalysts - Nature Energy
text:
AbstractManipulating surface strain via lattice mismatch can enhance electrocatalytic activity in epitaxial transition metal films, but long-term performance remains challenging. Although thick films can improve stability against dissolution, they may suffer from irreversible strain relaxation, reducing catalytic activity. Here using epitaxial platinum films for the electrochemical oxygen reduction reaction, we demonstrate thickness-dependent critical strain below which irreversible strain relaxation is avoided, defining the thicknesses range that optimizes catalyst stability and activity. First principles calculations reveal that the critical strain values range from −8.2% (compressive) to 2.7% (tensile) and the optimal strain (−2.5%) is maintained in Pt films up to ~3-nm thick. In H2–air polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells, Pt films on iridium deliver a mass activity of 1.5 ± 0.3 A mgPt−1 at 0.9 V and less than 10% performance loss after 30,000 cycles, compared with less than 0.4 A mgPt−1 and more than 60% performance loss for Pt and PtNi catalysts.

(D, E, F) actual EDS spectra from vacuum region, the outmost 1 nm of Pt(111) film, and Ir(111) bulk, respectively. It is indicated in C for the regions used for the measurement of actual EDS spectra. The measurement of the vacuum region (D) shows shifted Ir background noise, which suggests that Ir signal in the outmost 1 nm of Pt(111) is largely from the background noise.Extended Data Fig. 5 XAS measurement of UTF Pt/Ir.The Pt loading is 19 µgPt/cm2. See Supplementary Table 2 for details.Extended Data Fig. 6 Electrochemical measurement of UTF xPt/26Ir in MEA.Pt surface loadings on 26 µgIr/cm2 base are 1, 4, 9, 19, and 31 µgPt/cm2, respectively. (A) ORR absolute activity, (B) ORR Pt mass activity. Absolute activity refers to the measured current density at 0.900 VMeasured vs RHE, with corrections for parasitic shorting and H2 crossover current densities (estimated by cyclic voltammetry with N2 in place of O2). Pt mass activity is the absolute activity divided by the areal Pt loading, that is 1, 4, 9, 19, and 31 µgPt/cm2, respectively.Extended Data Fig. 7 Specific activity (A) and electrochemical surface area (B) of UTF xPt/26Ir catalysts.As a comparison, specific activities and surface area of Pt and PtNi are also given.Extended Data Fig. 8 STEM measurement of a Pt/Ir UTF sample after the accelerated stress test.(A) high-resolution STEM measurement, which shows pseudomorphic interface. (B) line profile of an atomic layer of Pt film. The measurements were calibrated using AuPd nanoparticles with a known lattice constant (that is, 0.400 nm).
